title: Bias in LLM Judges
concept: LLM judges suffer from biases: egocentric bias (prefers its own writing style), position bias (prefers whichever response is shown first), and verbosity bias (prefers longer answers).

exercises.prompt: What is a major challenge when using LLMs as judges to evaluate text?

options.text: They are completely neutral and never make mistakes.
They suffer from biases like preferring longer or first-placed answers.
They only grade math equations, never written essays.

exercises.explain: LLM judges are AI models used to evaluate other texts, but they suffer from biases like preferring longer answers, the first option, or their own style.

cards.title: Can We Trust AI Judges?
cards.body: We often use AI to grade and judge other AIs because it's fast and cheap. But did you know that AI judges have human-like flaws? They can be tricked by how long an answer is, which answer they read first, or even if the answer sounds like something they would write! Let's learn how to spot these sneaky biases.

cards.title: Meet the Biased Judges
cards.body:
An LLM judge is an AI used to grade and compare different text responses. However, they struggle with three main biases: 
1. **Egocentric Bias**: The AI prefers answers written in its own style.
2. **Position Bias**: The AI prefers the first response it reads.
3. **Verbosity Bias**: The AI prefers longer, wordier answers over concise ones.

cards.title: The Cake Judge Analogy
cards.body: Imagine you ask a judge to taste-test two chocolate cakes. But this judge has a bias: they will automatically pick whichever cake is placed on the left side of the table, regardless of taste! That is position bias. Or they might pick the cake decorated in their own baking style (egocentric bias), or simply the biggest cake on the plate (verbosity bias). Just like this biased human, LLM judges can make unfair decisions based on silly patterns instead of quality.

cards.title: Bias in Action
cards.body:
Imagine we ask an AI judge to pick the best summary of a movie. 

- Response A is short and perfect.
- Response B is long, rambling, and repeats itself.

Because of **verbosity bias**, the LLM judge might choose Response B simply because it has more words! 

If we swap them and put Response B first, it is even more likely to win because of **position bias**.

cards.title: Myth: AI Judges are Neutral
cards.body: Some people think that since AI is just code, it must be a perfectly objective and fair judge. But this is a myth! LLMs are trained on human text, so they inherit human-like flaws. They are highly susceptible to order, length, and style, making them unfair judges if we don't actively correct their biases.

cards.title: Human vs. LLM Judge
cards.body: Let's compare how a human judge and a biased LLM judge evaluate the exact same set of answers. Notice what features grab the AI's attention!

right.text: Easily distracted by which answer is first, how long it is, and if it uses its favorite words.

left.text: Focuses on accuracy, truthfulness, and how well the question was answered. Length doesn't trick them.

exercises.prompt: An AI judge created by Company X consistently gives higher scores to answers written by Company X's chatbot. Which bias does this show?
exercises.explain: Egocentric bias occurs when an AI model rates a response higher simply because the writing style matches its own.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Position bias means an LLM judge is more likely to choose an answer simply because it is shown first in the prompt.
exercises.explain: Position bias makes the AI judge prefer whichever response is presented first, regardless of its quality.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe when an AI judge prefers longer text.
exercises.sentence: When an AI judge favors a long, wordy answer over a short one, it is showing ________ bias.
exercises.explain: Verbosity bias is the tendency of AI judges to score longer, wordier answers higher than short, concise ones.

exercises.prompt: Which of these instructions helps a developer reduce bias in an LLM judge?

options.text: 'Tell me which response is better, and make sure it is at least 500 words long.'
'Compare these two responses. Ignore their length, and evaluate them twice, swapping their order.'
'Choose the first response because it matches your writing style perfectly.'

exercises.explain: The prompt in Option B actively instructs the LLM to ignore length (fighting verbosity bias) and suggests swapping positions (fighting position bias) to ensure fairness.

exercises.prompt: You are designing a system that uses an LLM to grade student essays. How can you protect your system from both position bias and verbosity bias?

options.text: Letting the LLM write longer responses and keeping the same order every time.
Swapping the order of responses and enforcing strict length limits.
Only using the LLM's favorite writing style for all candidate answers.
Asking the LLM to write a third, even longer response to break the tie.

exercises.explain: Verbosity bias makes LLM judges favor longer answers, while egocentric bias makes them prefer texts that match their own training style. To prevent these biases from ruining our evaluations, we can swap the order of the answers (fixing position bias) and set strict word limits (fixing verbosity bias).
